knowledge.title: Pistol round: Armor vs full utility
knowledge.description: Buy kevlar + P250 + one nade as a default. Skip armor only if your role is pure entry on a fast rush where the first duel decides the round. Armor reduces damage from a P2000/USP body shot from 24 to 16 — enough to win one extra exchange per pistol round on average across a long sample.
knowledge.situation: Pistol round, deciding between armor and utility
knowledge.pro_example: Tier-1 meta: ~85% of CTs and ~70% of Ts buy armor on pistol when not committed to a 5-stack rush

knowledge.title: Anti-eco discipline: do not over-peek
knowledge.description: When the enemy is on a save (no rifles), the rifle side should NOT push aggressively into hold positions. Anti-eco losses are economy double-hits: you give them a free $3250 reward AND lose 1-2 rifles. Hold defaults at default angles, trade in 2s, and let them come to you.
knowledge.situation: Holding versus a force/eco round, full rifles
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: anti-eco round win rate drops below 70% when CTs over-rotate or stack one site

knowledge.title: Force buy: round 3 after pistol loss + lost conversion
knowledge.description: If you lose pistol AND lose the round-2 anti-eco (0-2), force buy with armor + Galil/FAMAS + a flash. Goal is NOT to win — it's to deal $3000+ damage and deny the enemy round-3 full buy. A force win flips momentum; a force loss costs you nothing because you were already broken.
knowledge.situation: Down 0-2, broke economy, deciding force vs save
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: round 3 force success ~30%, but enemy economy suppression converts ~55% of round 4s

knowledge.title: Save round discipline: no individual heroics
knowledge.description: On a save round, exit-frag from off-angles only if it costs zero risk. Do not push for ego frags. Saving even one rifle changes next round from a force buy to a half buy (rifle for one player), which raises the team's expected round-4 win probability by ~15%.
knowledge.situation: Save round, weapon retention priority
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: rifle retention rate on save rounds is ~65% for tier-1 teams, ~45% for tier-3

knowledge.title: Crosshair placement: head level, pre-aimed at angles
knowledge.description: Keep your crosshair at head height and pre-aimed at the next angle you will see. The single largest source of lost duels at intermediate skill is crosshair re-targeting time. If your crosshair is already on the angle when the enemy appears, your reaction time effectively drops by 100-150 ms.
knowledge.situation: Holding angles, clearing corners, low headshot percentage
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: top AWPers maintain head-level crosshair >95% of the time when moving through known sightlines

knowledge.title: Spray control: first 7 bullets are everything
knowledge.description: AK and M4 sprays are decisive in the first 7 bullets. Practice pulling down hard then slightly left for 3 bullets, then drift right. After 10 bullets, accuracy collapses — burst tap the rest. 90% of spray kills happen inside the first 7 bullets at <20m range.
knowledge.situation: Losing close-range rifle duels, low spray accuracy
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 AK spray accuracy in first 7 bullets is ~80% at 10m

knowledge.title: Counter-strafe before every shot
knowledge.description: Tap the opposite movement key for one frame before shooting to instantly stop your model. Without counter-strafe your first bullet has up to 10× the inaccuracy spread. This is the single highest-leverage mechanical habit at intermediate skill levels.
knowledge.situation: Missing first shots while peeking, low first-bullet accuracy
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 100% of tier-1 players counter-strafe; the habit is automatic after a few months of practice

knowledge.title: Trade kills > survival in 2v2 retakes
knowledge.description: In a retake, the second player's job is to trade the first player's death within 2 seconds, NOT to stay alive. A traded death turns a 1v2 into a 1v1 with bomb time pressure on the enemy. Untraded deaths bleed retake win rate from ~50% to ~15%.
knowledge.situation: CT retake, deciding whether to push together or stagger
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 2v2 retakes with successful trade win ~52%, untraded retakes win ~17%

knowledge.title: Mid-round calling: information triggers, not impulses
knowledge.description: Mid-round calls should be based on hard information triggers — confirmed enemy positions, utility usage, or timing reads — not gut feelings. Define your team's triggers before the round (e.g., 'no smoke on connector by 0:50 = rotate B'). Impulse rotates leak round win rate.
knowledge.situation: IGL deciding to rotate or commit mid-round
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 IGLs make ~3 mid-round calls per round, ~80% based on a confirmed trigger

knowledge.title: Default setup: 2-1-2 by default, adapt by buy
knowledge.description: On a default round with full buys both sides, the standard CT setup is 2 A, 1 mid/connector, 2 B. Adapt by economy: if Ts are on a force, stack the most valuable site (the one they typically hit on forces). If CTs are on a force, flatten utility on chokes and play for time.
knowledge.situation: CT default setup, deciding spread
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 2-1-2 is the most common CT base on 6 of 7 active duty maps

knowledge.title: Damage callouts: number, location, weapon
knowledge.description: Always call damage as 'NN damage, location, weapon' — e.g. '85 in 2, long, AK'. Teammates use this to make immediate decisions: a 15 HP enemy is a free trade target. Generic 'one shot' callouts waste 1-2 seconds of decision-making time per engagement.
knowledge.situation: Team coordination after a damage trade
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 IGL teams make damage callouts on >90% of confirmed hits

knowledge.title: Utility economy: do not waste smokes on contact
knowledge.description: Smokes are the highest-value utility in the buy menu ($300 each). Throwing a smoke after first contact, where it cuts no rotation and isolates no angle, is a wasted $300. Save smokes for execute timings, post-plant cutoffs, or pre-fire denial of common sightlines.
knowledge.situation: Mid-round, deciding whether to throw a smoke reactively
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: average smoke cost per kill is ~$120 for tier-1, ~$210 for tier-3 — efficiency matters

knowledge.title: Post-plant: hold the angle the bomb plant favors
knowledge.description: After plant, your job is NOT to hunt — it's to hold the angle that forces the CT to defuse from the worst possible position. On most plants there is one 'lurk angle' where you can pre-fire the defuser. Find it for each common plant spot and rehearse it.
knowledge.situation: T post-plant, deciding where to hold
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: ~70% of post-plant T wins come from a single pre-aimed lurk angle

knowledge.title: Bomb plant placement: maximize defuse exposure
knowledge.description: Always plant where the defuser is exposed to your hold angle, not where it's fastest to plant. A 'safe plant' that lets the CT defuse behind cover is an automatic round loss in 2v3 and worse situations. The 1-second extra plant time is worth it.
knowledge.situation: T executing, deciding plant spot
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: optimal plant spots improve post-plant win rate by 8-12 percentage points

knowledge.title: 1vX clutch: separate them with time, not space
knowledge.description: In a clutch, the goal is to create time gaps between the enemies — not necessarily distance. Use a corner that forces them to cross a chokepoint one at a time. Sound discipline (walk!) and isolation are worth more than aim in 1v3+.
knowledge.situation: 1vX clutch scenario, low clutch win rate
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 1v2 clutch win rate is ~22% for tier-1, dropping to ~8% in 1v3

knowledge.title: AWPer positioning: one shot, then reposition
knowledge.description: After firing your AWP, immediately reposition. The enemy team now knows your angle and will throw utility or peek with numbers. Holding the same angle for a second shot is the #1 cause of AWPer deaths at intermediate skill.
knowledge.situation: Playing AWP, dying after the second peek
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: top AWPers reposition within 3 seconds of firing >85% of the time

knowledge.title: Movement: walk at every information-sensitive moment
knowledge.description: Walking is silent. Running is broadcast. Walk in the last 5 meters of any rotation, in any post-plant approach, and any time you do not want to give a free position read. The 1-second walk window is worth more than the 1-second time save of running.
knowledge.situation: Rotating or repositioning, getting picked off
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 players walk for ~30% of round time, vs ~10% for tier-3

knowledge.title: Utility usage timing: stagger, do not stack
knowledge.description: When executing a site, stagger your utility over 4-6 seconds: smoke first, then molly, then flash on entry. Stacking all utility in the same second wastes the cover window and gives the CT a single 'wait' moment instead of three decision points.
knowledge.situation: T execute, utility stacking up
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: optimal utility stagger improves entry frag rate by ~12%

knowledge.title: Map control: slow defaults beat fast hits on equal economy
knowledge.description: When both teams are full buy, slow defaults (60-80 second build-up with map control) outperform fast hits by ~8 percentage points. Fast hits work as a punish or against overstacks — not as a default strategy.
knowledge.situation: T-side, full buy, choosing pace
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams use fast hits on ~15% of full-buy rounds; the rest are slow defaults

knowledge.title: Smoke timing: throw before you need cover, not after
knowledge.description: Smokes have a 1.5s deploy time — they only block vision after fully expanding. Throwing a smoke as you're already in the open is too late; you'll die before it covers you. Pre-throw smokes 2 seconds before you need them.
knowledge.situation: Dying while smoke is still expanding
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: optimal smoke deploys land 2.0-2.5s before the cover is needed

knowledge.title: Trading discipline: stand close enough to get the trade
knowledge.description: If you're more than ~5 meters from your teammate when they peek, you cannot trade them in time. Common error: entry fragger pushes 10m ahead while support 'covers' from too far back. Stay tight on entries.
knowledge.situation: Entry fraggers dying without trades
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: average trade distance for successful trades is ~4m, ~7m for failed trades

knowledge.title: Eco round exit fragging: one player, one angle
knowledge.description: On an eco, send one player to a single off-angle for an exit frag. Sending multiple players for ego frags loses 2-3 weapons that could have been saved. Pro teams treat eco rounds as economy management, not gambling.
knowledge.situation: Eco round, deciding exit frag commitment
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 eco round exit frag commitment is ~1 player on ~60% of ecos

knowledge.title: Bomb time management: 35-second plant rule
knowledge.description: Plant the bomb before 0:35 remaining. Earlier plants give you a full 40-second post-plant where the CT must come to you. Plants after 0:35 leave too little time for the bomb to tick down — you may have to defend a retake under time pressure.
knowledge.situation: T execute timing, slow plants
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: post-plant win rate drops ~15% for plants after 0:30

knowledge.title: CT rotation: rotate on confirmation, not suspicion
knowledge.description: Do not rotate off your site based on a single sound cue. Rotate when at least two cues confirm the enemy commitment (e.g., utility usage + footsteps + damage on a teammate). False rotates leak ~20% of CT rounds at intermediate skill.
knowledge.situation: CT-side, deciding to rotate
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 CT rotation accuracy (correct rotates / total rotates) is >85%

knowledge.title: First contact economics: do not commit utility on a pick
knowledge.description: When your AWPer takes a pick, do NOT immediately follow up with smokes/flashes. The pick has already created the advantage; spending utility doubles down on a round you've already won. Save the utility for the next site execute.
knowledge.situation: After a successful opening pick
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams use ~30% less utility on rounds with a successful opening pick

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: read the opponent default, counter next round
knowledge.description: After losing a round, study what the opponent ran. Did they default slow or rush? Did they commit utility to a specific chokepoint? If they ran the same A execute twice, stack A next round. Most teams repeat their highest-confidence call under pressure — punish it.
knowledge.situation: Lost two rounds to the same setup, no adjustment made
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 IGLs adjust defensive positioning after 2 consecutive executes to the same site

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: punish repetitive utility usage
knowledge.description: If the enemy smokes the same chokepoint 3+ rounds in a row, push through it next round with a flash. Repetitive utility becomes predictable timing — your team can time a push to arrive as the smoke blooms. The smoke is wasted and you win a numbers advantage.
knowledge.situation: Enemy keeps smoking the same angle every round
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams counter-push through predictable smokes ~35% of the time after identifying the pattern

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: counter fast rushes with anti-flash positioning
knowledge.description: Against fast B rushes, play with your back to the wall so entry flashes do not blind you. Pre-aim the entry chokepoint from a position where the flash trajectory cannot hit your field of view. Trade the first entry for 2 kills before repositioning.
knowledge.situation: Enemy keeps fast-rushing a site with flashes
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: anti-flash positions reduce flash-blinded deaths by ~60% on average

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: counter aggressive AWP with utility chains
knowledge.description: If the enemy AWP keeps getting opening picks from an aggressive angle, throw a molotov or HE grenade at the angle before your team peeks. Follow with a smoke to cut the retreat. The AWP either takes damage, repositions, or is trapped behind utility.
knowledge.situation: Enemy AWPer keeps getting early picks from the same spot
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: double utility (molly + smoke) clears an aggressive AWP angle ~80% of the time

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: counter lurk plays with information utility
knowledge.description: If the enemy has a player who consistently lurks behind your rotate, throw a flash or smoke into the lurk path after the execute starts. Even better: leave one player anchored with a crossfire on the expected lurk route. Denying the lurk turns their 4v5 site hit into a real 4v5.
knowledge.situation: Getting killed by a lurker during site retake
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 CTs assign a dedicated lurk-watcher when they identify a consistent lurk pattern

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: counter eco rushes with layered crossfire
knowledge.description: On anti-eco, do NOT push into the enemy. Set up a 3-deep crossfire at the chokepoint they are most likely to rush. First player gets 1-2, second trades, third mops up. Even a 5-man eco rush fails against a properly layered crossfire.
knowledge.situation: Losing to enemy eco round rushes with full buy
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: anti-eco round win rate exceeds 85% when CTs hold default positions with crossfire

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: adapt to enemy utility expenditure mid-round
knowledge.description: Track how much utility the enemy has used in the first 30 seconds. If they dumped 4+ utility on A without executing, they are faking — hold B positions firm. If they spent zero utility, expect a late-round fast hit with full nades.
knowledge.situation: CT-side, unsure if the T utility usage means commitment or fake
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 4+ utility spent without execute = fake ~70% of the time at tier-1

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: counter stack defenses with fast splits
knowledge.description: If the CT keeps stacking 3 on one site, split from two sides simultaneously. A split forces the 3-stack to choose which angle to cover — one will always be open. Timing is everything: both entry paths must arrive within 2 seconds of each other.
knowledge.situation: T-side, enemy keeps stacking one site with 3 players
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: well-timed splits against 3-stacks have ~65% plant rate, vs ~40% for single-entry executes

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: counter aggressive CT pushes with passive holds
knowledge.description: If the CT keeps pushing out of their site early, hold a passive off-angle and let them walk into your crosshair. Aggressive CTs expect to find you at default angles — a 10-meter deeper hold turns their aggression into a free kill.
knowledge.situation: T-side, CT keeps pushing out of the site early in the round
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: passive holds against aggressive CTs convert ~70% of first contacts

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: use tactical timeouts to break momentum
knowledge.description: When the enemy wins 3+ rounds in a row with the same strat, call a tactical timeout. The timeout breaks their rhythm and gives you time to explicitly plan a counter. A well-timed timeout reverses 3-round streaks ~45% of the time.
knowledge.situation: Losing consecutive rounds, enemy has momentum
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams use tactical timeouts after 3-round losing streaks ~60% of the time

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: identify and counter force buy patterns
knowledge.description: Most teams have a default force buy strat — usually a fast execute with pistol + utility. Scout for it: if they forced Galil + armor round 3, expect an A execute with 2 flashes. Position to deny the entry path they always use on force rounds.
knowledge.situation: Losing to enemy force buys repeatedly
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: force buy executes hit the same site >60% of the time per team — study the pattern

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: your job is to die for information, not survive
knowledge.description: The entry fragger creates space by being first through the door. Your job is to get the first frag OR die in a tradeable position. If you die but your support trades it, the team is up 4v4 with site control — that is a win for you.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger dies without getting a kill, feels useless
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: top entry fraggers have ~48% opening duel win rate — the trade is what matters

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: always peek with utility, never dry
knowledge.description: A dry peek against a holding CT gives them ~200ms advantage (they are pre-aimed, you must acquire). A flash before your peek reverses this — they turn away, you have a free kill window. Never dry peek unless you are counter-strafing a known weak angle.
knowledge.situation: Losing opening duels as entry, low opening kill rate
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: entry frags with flash assist have ~62% success rate vs ~41% dry peek

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: wide swing vs shoulder peek
knowledge.description: Wide swing when you have a flash covering you — the momentum catches the CT off guard. Shoulder peek when scouting for utility or checking if the angle is held. Never wide swing into an unknown angle without information — that is gambling, not entry fragging.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger, choosing peek style
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: wide swings with utility win ~58% of duels; wide swings without utility win ~35%

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: call your angle before peeking
knowledge.description: Before you peek, tell your support exactly which angle you will clear. 'I'm swinging left, you trade right.' This ensures the trade is instant. Without a call, the support player guesses — and guessing wastes the 1-second trade window.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger dying without being traded
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: pre-called entries have ~75% trade rate vs ~50% without a call

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: after getting the frag, call and plant
knowledge.description: After winning the opening duel, immediately call the site clear level and plant the bomb. Do not chase the second kill — your job transitions from entry to plant. Every second of delay gives the CT time to rotate. Plant > frag in the post-entry window.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger wins opening duel but does not plant
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: bomb plant within 5 seconds of entry frag increases round win rate by ~12%

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: pistol round entry is different
knowledge.description: On pistol, entry frags are about momentum, not trading. Lead the rush with a flash, strafe-peek the first angle, and rely on pistol headshots. The entry fragger on pistol should buy armor + pistol, NOT utility — your support buys the flashes.
knowledge.situation: Pistol round, entry fragger role
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: pistol round entry fraggers buy kevlar + P250 ~80% of the time

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: spacing is everything — stay within trade distance
knowledge.description: The entry fragger should be 3-5 meters ahead of the support. Too close: both die to the same spray. Too far: the trade is impossible. The support player should see the entry fragger's back at all times during the push.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger dying too far from support
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: optimal entry-support spacing is 3-5 meters; trade success drops 30% beyond 7m

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: eco role — sacrifice play
knowledge.description: On eco rounds, the entry fragger's job is to close the distance and get a pistol kill for a weapon pickup. Prioritize getting close to the enemy without dying at range. Run through smokes, flash yourself in, and force a close-range duel where the pistol has a chance.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger on eco round
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: eco entry success rate at <5m range is ~35% vs ~12% at >15m

knowledge.title: AWP: economic discipline — $4750 is a team investment
knowledge.description: The AWP costs $4750. If you die and lose it, the team loses a full-buy rifle's worth of economy. Before buying, ask: can the team afford to lose this weapon? If the team is on a thin buy, a rifle + utility is more valuable than an AWP with no utility.
knowledge.situation: Deciding whether to buy AWP on a borderline economy
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 AWPers skip the AWP on ~25% of buyable rounds due to team economy

knowledge.title: AWP: passive vs aggressive positioning
knowledge.description: Passive AWP (holding a deep angle) wins ~65% of duels against a peeking enemy. Aggressive AWP (pushing forward for a pick) wins ~55% but gives the enemy more info if you die. Default passive; go aggressive only when you need information or a momentum swing.
knowledge.situation: AWP player, choosing between aggressive and passive style
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: passive AWP positions average ~0.8 kills per death, aggressive average ~0.6

knowledge.title: AWP: early round picks — calculated gamble
knowledge.description: An early AWP pick (first 15 seconds) gives the team a massive advantage. But if you die, the team plays 4v5 with a rifle deficit. Take early picks only from positions where you can reposition into cover within 1 second of firing.
knowledge.situation: AWP player, deciding whether to take an aggressive early peek
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: early AWP picks succeed ~45% of the time; the risk-reward is positive if the AWP survives on miss

knowledge.title: AWP: reposition after every shot
knowledge.description: After firing, the enemy knows your position. Hold the same angle = die to a coordinated peek, a flash, or a trade. Move to a secondary angle within 3 seconds. Have 2-3 pre-planned AWP positions per site — cycle between them.
knowledge.situation: AWP player dying after taking a shot
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 AWPers reposition within 3s of firing >85% of the time

knowledge.title: AWP: off-angle surprise factor
knowledge.description: Hold unexpected angles that the enemy does not pre-aim. An off-angle gives you 200-400ms of free aim time because the enemy's crosshair is pointed elsewhere. But off-angles only work once per half — after the enemy sees it, pre-aim it next round.
knowledge.situation: AWP player using the same angle every round
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: off-angle AWP first kill success rate is ~70% on first use, ~40% on repeat use same half

knowledge.title: AWP: T-side create space, do not overpeek
knowledge.description: On T-side, the AWP creates space by holding a sightline that prevents CT aggression. You do not need to get kills — just holding the angle forces the CT to play passive. Peek for picks only when the team is ready to capitalize on the advantage.
knowledge.situation: T-side AWP, feeling useless without kills
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: T-side AWP space creation (holding a sightline) denies CT info plays in ~75% of rounds

knowledge.title: AWP: save priority — never lose the AWP
knowledge.description: If the round is clearly lost, save the AWP immediately. Do not fight for a heroic kill. An AWP saved is $4750 the team does not have to spend next round. Run to a corner, hide, and survive. Every lost AWP on a lost round costs the team an average of $2000 in extra buys.
knowledge.situation: Round is lost, holding AWP in danger
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 AWPers save their weapon on lost rounds ~80% of the time

knowledge.title: AWP: retake discipline — hold the angle, do not push
knowledge.description: On retake, the AWP holds the longest angle and lets rifle players entry. The AWP's one-shot kill is maximized at range, not in close-quarters chaos. Cover the plant area from as far back as possible and let your teammates clear the close angles.
knowledge.situation: AWP in a retake, pushing into the site
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: AWP retake kills average at 15-25m range; AWP dies within 5m on >60% of retake pushes

knowledge.title: Support: your flashes win rounds, not your frags
knowledge.description: The support player's primary weapon is utility, not the rifle. Throw flashes for your entry fragger, smoke the rotate path, molly the close angle. If your entry gets 2 kills off your flash, you contributed more than any frag would have.
knowledge.situation: Support player feeling useless with low kill count
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: top support players average 4.5 flash assists per match, directly enabling ~35% of team's entry kills

knowledge.title: Support: time your flash with entry movement
knowledge.description: Throw the flash so it pops as your entry fragger is mid-swing. Too early: the CT recovers before your entry arrives. Too late: your entry is already dead. Practice the timing — a well-timed flash is the single highest-impact play for a support player.
knowledge.situation: Support player flash timing off, entry dying before flash pops
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: optimal flash pop time is 0.3-0.5s before the entry commits to the swing

knowledge.title: Support: fill the gap after entry death
knowledge.description: When your entry fragger dies, you become the next entry. Do not hesitate — the momentum window is 2-3 seconds. Use the information from the entry's death (angle, position) to pre-aim and finish the fight. Hesitation turns a traded kill into a lost round.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger dies, support player freezes
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: trade kills happen within 2s of entry death in >75% of successful trade scenarios

knowledge.title: Support: trade kills are your primary stat
knowledge.description: Track your trade kill rate, not your K/D. A support player with 15 kills and 12 trade kills is vastly more valuable than one with 20 kills and 3 trade kills. Trade kills maintain round advantage; solo kills often just delay the inevitable.
knowledge.situation: Support player focused on K/D ratio
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 support players have ~65% trade kill rate; below 50% indicates positioning errors

knowledge.title: Support: economy priority — full utility over a better weapon
knowledge.description: If you have $4200, buy an M4 + no utility, or a FAMAS + full utility? Buy the FAMAS + utility. Your utility enables the team's entry — without it, the entry fragger faces a dry peek. A support without utility is just a worse rifler.
knowledge.situation: Support player buying weapon over utility on tight economy
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: support players prioritize full utility on ~85% of tier-1 force buys

knowledge.title: Support: anchor retake utility
knowledge.description: On CT side, the support player saves 1 smoke and 1 molly for retake. Do not dump all utility on the initial defense — the retake requires utility to isolate angles and clear the plant area. A support with retake utility raises team retake win rate by ~15%.
knowledge.situation: CT retake with no utility remaining
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 CTs retain 40-50% of total utility for retake scenarios

knowledge.title: Support: post-plant smoke timing
knowledge.description: After plant on T-side, the support's job is to smoke the defuse position at the right time. Throw too early: smoke fades before defuse. Throw too late: CT defuses behind it. Time the smoke to bloom when the CT is most likely to start the defuse — usually 8-12 seconds after plant.
knowledge.situation: T post-plant, support player unsure when to smoke
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: optimal post-plant smoke timing is 8-15 seconds after plant, covering the 40s bomb timer

knowledge.title: Lurk: appear after the execute starts, not before
knowledge.description: The lurk player times their rotation to arrive behind the CT rotate path 5-10 seconds after the main execute begins. Too early: you get picked before the team commits. Too late: the retake is already set. The ideal timing catches rotating CTs mid-sprint.
knowledge.situation: Lurk player timing their rotation
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: optimal lurk timing is 5-10 seconds after execute starts; CTs in sprint mode are easiest targets

knowledge.title: Lurk: position behind the rotate path
knowledge.description: Set up where the CT will rotate through, not where they start. If your team hits A, the CT rotates from B through mid — hold mid. The lurk position should give you backs or flanks on rotating players, never face-to-face duels.
knowledge.situation: Lurk player, choosing where to hold
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: lurk kills from behind/flank have ~80% success rate vs ~45% from face-to-face

knowledge.title: Lurk: report information without engaging
knowledge.description: If you see 2 CTs rotating, call it immediately but do NOT shoot. The information is worth more than the kill: your team now knows the retake numbers. Shoot only when the timing guarantees a kill AND survival, or when your team needs the frag to win.
knowledge.situation: Lurk player spotting rotating CTs
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: lurk info calls that prevent over-rotation save more rounds than lurk kills on average

knowledge.title: Lurk: discipline — do NOT chase kills
knowledge.description: After getting a lurk kill, do NOT chase the second. Reposition immediately. The enemy now knows your location. Chasing turns a successful lurk play into a traded death 60% of the time. Get one, hide, and let the numbers advantage play out.
knowledge.situation: Lurk player chasing kills after getting one
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: lurk players who reposition after first kill have 2.1x survival rate vs chasers

knowledge.title: Lurk: fake to draw a rotation
knowledge.description: Throw a flash + a decoy or fire a few shots at a chokepoint to make the CTs rotate toward you — then go silent. Your team hits the now-weakened site. The fake lurk is one of the most effective mid-round calls when the CTs are overstacking one site.
knowledge.situation: T-side, CTs are stacking one site heavily
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: fake lurk plays draw at least one CT rotate ~55% of the time at tier-1

knowledge.title: Lurk: sound discipline — walk everywhere
knowledge.description: The lurk player walks for 90% of the round. Any running sound gives away your position and kills the surprise. You are a ghost until the moment you appear. Even when time is low, walking 5 more seconds to maintain silence is worth the trade-off.
knowledge.situation: Lurk player getting spotted due to sound cues
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 lurk players walk >80% of round time; sound discipline is their #1 skill

knowledge.title: IGL: read enemy economy, plan the round accordingly
knowledge.description: Before the round starts, calculate the enemy's expected economy. Are they on a full buy, force, or save? This determines your round plan: play aggressive vs saves, default vs full buys, anti-force vs forces. An IGL who gets the economy read wrong wastes the team's entire round.
knowledge.situation: IGL planning the round without checking enemy economy
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 IGLs make correct economy reads ~90% of the time; tier-3 ~65%

knowledge.title: IGL: call by information, not by script
knowledge.description: Scripted calls work for the first 3 rounds. After that, the enemy adapts. The IGL must shift from script to reads: what did we see? Where did they spend utility? Where are they NOT? Real-time reads beat rehearsed plays by round 5 of any half.
knowledge.situation: IGL running the same play every round, getting countered
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 IGLs use scripted calls in ~20% of rounds; the rest are read-based adaptations

knowledge.title: IGL: manage team tilt
knowledge.description: When the team loses 3+ rounds and energy drops, the IGL must address it directly. Call a timeout, simplify the next call ('just do defaults, play your game'), or tell the star player to make a play. Ignoring tilt compounds it. One calm sentence from the IGL resets the mood.
knowledge.situation: Team on a losing streak, morale dropping
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: teams that call timeout within 1 round of 3-streak reverse the momentum ~45% of the time

knowledge.title: IGL: default then read then call sequence
knowledge.description: Every round should follow this loop: (1) run the default setup to gather info, (2) read what the info tells you, (3) call the execute based on the read. Calling the execute before the default finishes wastes info. Calling no execute after the default wastes time.
knowledge.situation: IGL calling executes too early or too late
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 T-side rounds average 45-60 seconds of default play before execute commitment

knowledge.title: IGL: mid-round adaptation triggers
knowledge.description: Define explicit triggers before the round: 'if no smoke on mid by 0:50, hit B.' 'If we get a pick, fast rotate to the opened site.' 'If they push our lurk, go 4-stack opposite site.' Triggers remove hesitation from mid-round calls.
knowledge.situation: IGL freezing during mid-round decision
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 IGLs define 2-3 triggers per round; triggers fire on ~40% of rounds

knowledge.title: IGL: confidence in calls over perfect calls
knowledge.description: A bad call made with confidence is better than a perfect call made with hesitation. The team executes a confident call cleanly; a hesitant call causes desync, late utility, and staggered entries. Commit fully — you can fix the call next round, but you cannot fix a split team.
knowledge.situation: IGL second-guessing their own call mid-round
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: teams that commit to the IGL's call win ~8% more rounds than teams with mid-round indecision

knowledge.title: IGL: economy management — when to force
knowledge.description: Force buy when: (1) a win breaks the enemy's economy, (2) you have 2+ players with $2000+ and can get armor + pistol/SMG + some utility, (3) the scoreline demands it (e.g., 13-14). Do NOT force when: saving this round buys a full buy next round that you can win cleanly.
knowledge.situation: IGL deciding force buy vs full save
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 IGLs force buy ~30% of potential save rounds; force success rate ~25-35%

knowledge.title: IGL: set plays vs defaults ratio
knowledge.description: Run 2-3 set plays per half (specific utility + timing executes) and default the rest. Too many set plays makes you predictable; too few means you never have a rehearsed answer when the read is unclear. Defaults gather info, set plays close rounds.
knowledge.situation: IGL wondering how many set plays to prepare
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams run set plays on ~20-30% of rounds, defaults on the rest

knowledge.title: Clutch 1v1: time is your weapon
knowledge.description: In a 1v1, the player with more time wins. As CT: delay and force the T to peek you under bomb timer pressure. As T: plant and make the CT defuse under your crosshair. Never rush a 1v1 — use every second of the clock to force the opponent into a disadvantaged position.
knowledge.situation: 1v1 clutch scenario
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: the player who forces the other to peek first wins ~65% of 1v1 clutches

knowledge.title: Clutch 1v2: isolate them into two 1v1s
knowledge.description: In a 1v2, the goal is to separate the two enemies into individual fights. Use corners, sound, and bomb timer to create sequential 1v1s. If both enemies can see you at the same time, you lose every time. Isolate, fight one, reposition, fight the other.
knowledge.situation: 1v2 clutch scenario
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 1v2 clutch rate at tier-1 is ~22% when isolated into two 1v1s, ~8% when simultaneous

knowledge.title: Clutch 1v3: bomb time is everything
knowledge.description: In a 1v3 as T, plant the bomb immediately. You cannot kill all 3 in a firefight. Plant, hide, and let the bomb timer force them to defuse one at a time while you hold the angle. As CT in 1v3, play for time — do NOT push into 3 players.
knowledge.situation: 1v3 clutch scenario
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 1v3 T clutch with bomb planted wins ~12%; without plant wins ~3%

knowledge.title: Clutch as CT: use defuse fakes
knowledge.description: Tap the defuse for 0.5 seconds and immediately stop. The T will either peek to stop the defuse (giving you a fight on your terms) or wait (giving you free seconds). Use defuse fakes when you suspect the T is holding a close angle on the bomb.
knowledge.situation: CT clutch, T holding the bomb angle
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: defuse fakes draw out the T player ~40% of the time; the info gained is always valuable

knowledge.title: Clutch as T: plant position decides everything
knowledge.description: In a clutch, plant the bomb where you can watch the defuse from a single angle with cover. A bad plant forces you to expose yourself to check the defuse. The 3 seconds you spend choosing the right plant position are the most important 3 seconds of the round.
knowledge.situation: T clutch, choosing where to plant
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: optimal plant spot selection improves T clutch win rate by ~15% vs random plant

knowledge.title: Clutch: sound is your radar
knowledge.description: In clutch scenarios, every sound tells a story. Footsteps = position. Reload = timing window. Scope = AWP angle. Defuse sound = 5-second window. Walk slowly, listen actively, and let the enemy's sounds guide your decisions. The player with better sound reads wins.
knowledge.situation: Clutch, not using sound cues
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: clutch specialists use sound cues to pre-aim ~70% of their clutch kills

knowledge.title: Clutch: preserve utility for the endgame
knowledge.description: If the round is trending toward a clutch, stop throwing utility mid-round. A smoke in a 1v2 is worth 10x what it was in a 5v5. One well-placed smoke can isolate an enemy, block a sightline while you defuse, or fake a position. Save at least 1 nade for clutch time.
knowledge.situation: Running out of utility in clutch scenarios
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: clutch players with 1+ utility piece remaining win ~30% more clutches than those without

knowledge.title: Clutch: shoulder peek for info
knowledge.description: Before committing to a fight in a clutch, shoulder peek the angle. Expose your shoulder for 1 frame, then pull back. This baits the enemy into shooting (revealing their position) without taking damage. One shoulder peek can turn a blind fight into a pre-aimed kill.
knowledge.situation: Clutch, unsure where the enemy is
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: shoulder peeks bait an enemy shot ~50% of the time, providing free position info

knowledge.title: Clutch: know the bomb timer precisely
knowledge.description: The bomb timer is exactly 40 seconds. A defuse without kit takes 10 seconds. With kit: 5 seconds. This means: without kit, you must start defusing at 10s remaining. With kit, 5s. If the timer is at 7s and you have no kit, do NOT defuse — fight instead.
knowledge.situation: Misjudging bomb timer in clutch
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: failed defuses due to timer miscalculation account for ~8% of lost CT clutch rounds

knowledge.title: Clutch: mental composure under pressure
knowledge.description: In a clutch, slow your breathing and your crosshair. Panic aim loses clutches. Your mechanical skill does not change in a 1v3 — only your decision-making does. Take 1 extra second to think before every action. The player who panics less wins more clutches.
knowledge.situation: Panicking in clutch situations, making bad decisions
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 clutch specialists report that slowing down decision-making improves their clutch rate by ~20%

knowledge.title: Jiggle peeking: gather info without committing
knowledge.description: Tap A-D rapidly to expose your model for 1-2 frames at the edge of an angle. This reveals enemy positions without giving them a shootable target. Use jiggle peeks to scout angles before committing to a wide swing or a push. Every angle should be jiggle-checked first.
knowledge.situation: Peeking unknown angles, getting killed immediately
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 players jiggle peek ~80% of angles before committing to a fight

knowledge.title: Angle advantage: the wider angle wins
knowledge.description: The player holding the wider angle (further from the corner) sees the peeking player first due to perspective geometry. If you are close to a corner and the enemy swings wide, they see you 50-100ms before you see them. Play wider angles or swing aggressively to counteract.
knowledge.situation: Losing duels when holding tight angles
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: wide-angle holders win ~58% of equal-skill duels vs tight-angle holders

knowledge.title: Peekers advantage: online lag benefits the aggressor
knowledge.description: In online CS2, the peeking player has a 20-80ms advantage due to network latency. This means: if you are holding an angle, the peeker sees you before their model renders on your screen. Counter this by playing off-angles, jiggling, or shoulder-peeking to bait the shot.
knowledge.situation: Dying to peeks before seeing the enemy
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: peeker's advantage is real and documented; off-angles reduce its impact by ~40%

knowledge.title: Crossfire setup: two angles, one kill zone
knowledge.description: A crossfire places two players on different sides of the same kill zone. The enemy can only check one angle at a time — the other player gets a free shot. Crossfires multiply your holding power: a 2-player crossfire is worth ~3 single-angle holds.
knowledge.situation: Setting up site defense with two players
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: crossfire setups hold sites at ~78% success rate vs ~55% for parallel positioning

knowledge.title: Off-angle plays: surprise positioning
knowledge.description: An off-angle is a position the enemy does not pre-aim when entering the site. It gives you 200-400ms of free aim time. But off-angles only work 1-2 times before the enemy starts checking them. Rotate off-angles every 2-3 rounds to maintain the surprise.
knowledge.situation: CT defense, playing the same angle every round
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: off-angle first kill rate is ~65% first use, drops to ~35% by third use same half

knowledge.title: Molotov lineup: clear common positions without peeking
knowledge.description: Learn 2-3 molotov lineups per site that clear the most common close-hold positions. A molotov forces the CT out of cover without requiring you to peek. Combined with a flash, it creates a 3-second window where the site is partially cleared for free.
knowledge.situation: Dying to close-hold positions on site entry
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams use molotovs to clear close positions on ~75% of full-buy executes

knowledge.title: Double flash technique: pop flash into team flash
knowledge.description: Throw a pop flash (short fuse, high trajectory) followed by a second flash (longer fuse, different angle). The first flash forces the CT to turn away — the second catches them turning back. This 2-flash combo blinds for 3-4 seconds total, guaranteeing the entry.
knowledge.situation: Single flash not effective enough for entries
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: double flash entries succeed ~68% vs ~52% for single flash entries

knowledge.title: Economy: double eco into force buy comeback
knowledge.description: After two consecutive eco rounds (saving both), the team has $7000-8000 per player — enough for a full buy with full utility. This 'double eco into full' strategy is more reliable than a mid-round force that risks extending the deficit.
knowledge.situation: After losing pistol, deciding save strategy
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: double eco + full buy wins ~55% of the buy round; single force + eco + buy wins ~45%

knowledge.title: Economy: rifle drop priority
knowledge.description: When one teammate has an AK/M4 and another has a pistol, the better aimer gets the rifle — not the player who saved it. Ego drops (keeping the rifle because 'I saved it') cost the team an average of 2 rounds per half.
knowledge.situation: Teammate saved a rifle, deciding who uses it
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams always drop the rifle to the highest-rated player; no ego about saves

knowledge.title: Number advantage: play safe, trade time for frags
knowledge.description: When your team has a numbers advantage (e.g., 3v2), do NOT rush. Play slow, trade time for position, and force the disadvantaged team to come to you. Pushing into a 2v3 as the 3-player side is the #1 way to throw an advantage.
knowledge.situation: Team has numbers advantage but pushes and loses it
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 3v2 round win rate is ~80% when played patiently, ~60% when the advantage team pushes

knowledge.title: Retake: defuse kit priority
knowledge.description: If you are CT retaking with a kit, you have 5 seconds to defuse instead of 10. This changes the entire retake calculus: with a kit you can trade a teammate, wait 3 seconds, and still defuse. Without a kit, every second counts. Buy kits on every buy round.
knowledge.situation: CT retake, no defuse kits bought
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: CT teams with 3+ kits win retakes ~12% more often than teams with 0-1 kits

knowledge.title: Retake: utility before push, always
knowledge.description: Never dry retake a site. Smoke the plant, molly a common post-plant position, flash the entry. Even one piece of utility raises your retake success rate by 15-20%. Retaking without utility is gambling — retaking with utility is strategy.
knowledge.situation: CT dry retaking and losing
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: retakes with 2+ utility pieces succeed ~55%; dry retakes succeed ~25%

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: track enemy AWP player tendencies
knowledge.description: Every AWPer has 2-3 favorite angles per map. After 3-4 rounds, you know their default. Counter with: (1) utility the angle before peeking, (2) double peek to bait the shot, (3) avoid the angle entirely and hit the other site. Neutralizing the AWP wins the economy war.
knowledge.situation: Enemy AWPer consistently getting kills from the same positions
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 IGLs adjust utility usage against the enemy AWP after 2-3 data points

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: mirror the enemy if they are winning
knowledge.description: If the enemy's setup is beating your approach, mirror it. Copy their utility timing, their positioning, their aggression level. Mirroring forces them to counter their own strat — and since they designed it, they know the weaknesses. Use it until they adapt.
knowledge.situation: Losing to a specific enemy setup repeatedly, no counter found
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: mirroring the opponent's CT setup forces them to innovate, breaking their rhythm

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: read the CT setup on first contact
knowledge.description: Your first contact as entry tells the team everything: how many CTs are here, which angles they hold, whether they pushed or played passive. Call what you see instantly — even if you die, the callout wins the round for the team.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger making first contact but not calling info
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: entry fraggers who call info on death enable ~15% more round wins than silent deaths

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: play for opening picks on CT side too
knowledge.description: Entry fraggers are not T-only. On CT side, aggressive early peeks for opening picks create the same advantage. The CT entry goes for a pick in the first 15 seconds and immediately falls back to site — never overcommit on CT entry plays.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger passive on CT side
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: CT aggressive opening picks succeed ~40% and create a 5v4 advantage on successful rounds

knowledge.title: AWP: dual AWP setup coordination
knowledge.description: Two AWPs work only if they hold different sightlines covering each other's weakness. Both AWPs on the same angle = double investment for one kill. One A long, one B — or one passive, one aggressive. Communicate who holds which angle every round.
knowledge.situation: Team running double AWP, both dying to the same push
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: dual AWP setups with separate sightlines have ~70% combined survival rate per round

knowledge.title: AWP: no-scope range discipline
knowledge.description: At ranges under 5 meters, hip-fire the AWP. The no-scope accuracy is reasonable at point-blank. At 5-15 meters, quick-scope (scope + shoot immediately). Beyond 15 meters, hard scope. Most AWP close-range deaths come from trying to hard-scope at point-blank.
knowledge.situation: AWP player dying to close-range enemies
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: no-scope AWP kills average ~15% hit rate at <5m; quick-scope ~45% at 5-15m

knowledge.title: Support: call utility before throwing
knowledge.description: Always call 'flashing' or 'smoking' before you throw. A teammate mid-peek who gets blinded by your flash costs the team a life. The 0.5-second call gives teammates time to avert their eyes or position for the utility.
knowledge.situation: Support player blinding teammates with flash
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: called flashes reduce team-flash incidents by ~80%

knowledge.title: Support: learn 3 retake smokes per site
knowledge.description: Each site has 3 smoke positions that isolate the T post-plant: (1) cut the lurk path, (2) block the plant area sightline, (3) isolate the close corner. Learn these 3 smokes for every site on your map pool. This is the highest-ROI practice for support players.
knowledge.situation: Support player with no retake smoke knowledge
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: support players with retake smoke knowledge increase team retake win rate by ~18%

knowledge.title: Lurk: eco round lurk is your best role
knowledge.description: On eco rounds, the lurker's job is to find a hiding spot and survive with a rifle for the next round. While the team rushes and dies, the lurker saves the most expensive weapon. One saved rifle changes the next round's economy entirely.
knowledge.situation: Lurk player role on eco rounds
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: lurk players save rifles on eco rounds ~70% of the time at tier-1

knowledge.title: Lurk: CT-side lurk for info flanks
knowledge.description: On CT side, the lurk player rotates through flanking paths after the bomb is spotted. Instead of running directly to the site, take the long way around to catch T players from behind. CT lurk kills during retakes have the highest impact per frag in the game.
knowledge.situation: CT lurk player during retake
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: CT flank kills during retakes contribute to ~25% of successful retakes at tier-1

knowledge.title: IGL: map veto strategy — ban what you can't counter
knowledge.description: In the veto, ban the map where the enemy has the highest win rate that your team cannot counter. Do NOT ban your worst map if the enemy never picks it. Study the opponent's map pool and ban their best — not your worst.
knowledge.situation: IGL preparing map veto
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: optimal vetoes target the opponent's best map, not the team's worst; the difference is ~5% series win rate

knowledge.title: IGL: half-time adjustments based on data
knowledge.description: At half-time, review three things: (1) which site did we lose most often, (2) which enemy player is carrying and from where, (3) did our economy allow us to execute our strats? Adjust site stacks, counter the star player, and plan economy around what actually happened.
knowledge.situation: IGL making half-time adjustments
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams that make targeted half-time adjustments win the next 3 rounds ~55% of the time

knowledge.title: Clutch: ninja defuse conditions
knowledge.description: A ninja defuse works when: (1) the T has no visual on the bomb, (2) the defuse starts before the T can rotate, (3) smoke covers the defuse or the T is distracted. With kit, you need 5 seconds of uninterrupted defuse. Without kit, 10 seconds — ninja without kit is almost never viable.
knowledge.situation: Attempting ninja defuse, failing
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: ninja defuses succeed ~5% of the time; with kit in smoke, ~15%

knowledge.title: Clutch: use the bomb as bait
knowledge.description: In a T clutch, plant the bomb and use the ticking sound as bait. The CT must come to the bomb — you choose the angle. If time is low, plant and hide in the closest off-angle. If time is high, plant and rotate to a far angle for a surprise peek.
knowledge.situation: T clutch, deciding to plant vs fight
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: T clutch with bomb planted wins ~18% vs ~8% without plant

knowledge.title: HE grenade: aim for feet, not body
knowledge.description: HE grenades do maximum damage when they detonate at feet level. Throwing an HE at chest height reduces damage because the blast center is above the target. Aim low — throw at the ground where the enemy stands, not at the enemy directly.
knowledge.situation: HE grenades doing low damage
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: feet-level HE averages 65-80 damage vs 40-55 damage for chest-level throws

knowledge.title: Flash discipline: do not flash your own team
knowledge.description: Before throwing a flash, check if any teammate is looking toward the flash trajectory. A team-flash turns a 5v5 into a 4v5 for 2-3 seconds. Call 'flashing [location]' every time. Turn your back to friendly flashes — the 0.3s turn costs less than 2s of blindness.
knowledge.situation: Team flashing each other during executes
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: team flash rate is ~12% at tier-3, ~3% at tier-1

knowledge.title: Decoy grenade: not useless in CS2
knowledge.description: The CS2 decoy shows as a radar blip to enemies — it simulates a player presence. Throw a decoy + one flash at the fake site while the team hits the real site. The decoy appears as a fifth player on the enemy's radar, drawing a rotation for $50.
knowledge.situation: Ignoring decoy grenades entirely
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: decoy fakes combined with a flash draw a CT rotation ~25% of the time

knowledge.title: Economy: $16000 cap — stop saving
knowledge.description: When any player hits $16000 (the economy cap), they must buy everything: rifle, full utility, kit. Saving at max economy is wasted money. Drop rifles to teammates, buy full utility for the team. Capped players should be the team's primary weapon donors.
knowledge.situation: Team has max economy but still saving utility
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams spend 95%+ of capped player economy; never leave money on the table

knowledge.title: Economy: loss bonus tracking
knowledge.description: CS2 loss bonus increments: $1400, $1900, $2400, $2900, $3400. After 5 consecutive losses, the team earns $3400 per player. A win resets the bonus. Track the loss bonus to predict when the enemy can afford a buy after consecutive losses.
knowledge.situation: Surprised by enemy economy after losses
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 IGLs track loss bonus to within $200 accuracy every round

knowledge.title: Movement: bunny hop for speed, not for style
knowledge.description: Bunny hopping in CS2 preserves momentum from jumps — a successful chain of bhops moves you 10-15% faster than running. Use it for rotations, save rounds, and surprise peeks. But a missed bhop slows you to walking speed. Only bhop when speed is critical and failure is acceptable.
knowledge.situation: Slow rotations, not using movement techniques
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: bhop usage in saves and rotations is standard at tier-1, ~15% speed advantage

knowledge.title: Positioning: playing headglitch spots
knowledge.description: A headglitch is a position where only your head is visible to the enemy, but you can see their entire body. This gives you a massive accuracy advantage — they have a tiny target, you have a large one. Learn the headglitch spots on every site for your map pool.
knowledge.situation: Dying from exposed positions during defense
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: headglitch positions win duels ~70% of the time against equal-skill opponents at range

knowledge.title: Positioning: do not hold angles where you can be mollied
knowledge.description: If your position can be reached by a molotov, the enemy will molly you out within 2-3 rounds. Play positions where a molotov cannot reach you, or play positions where you can sidestep the molly without giving up the angle.
knowledge.situation: Getting mollied out of the same position every round
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: molly-proof positions have ~20% higher survival rate over the course of a half

knowledge.title: Mid-round: play the clock — time management
knowledge.description: Each round has 1:55. As T: plant before 0:35 to give a full 40s post-plant. As CT: deny plant until 0:30 to force a rushed execute. Time management is the #2 skill after aim at intermediate levels. Every second has value.
knowledge.situation: Rounds expiring without plant, or rushing without need
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams average bomb plant at 0:48 remaining; tier-3 teams at 0:32

knowledge.title: Mid-round: man advantage discipline
knowledge.description: Up one player? Play passive and force the disadvantaged team to push you. Up two players? You can afford to push one angle while holding the rest. Never push when you are down on numbers unless the bomb timer forces it.
knowledge.situation: Throwing man advantage by being aggressive
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 4v3 passive play wins ~82% of rounds; 4v3 aggressive play wins ~65%

knowledge.title: Retakes: prioritize site entry over frag hunting
knowledge.description: In a retake, the goal is to reach the bomb and defuse, not to kill every T. If you can isolate the bomb area with utility and defuse under cover, do it even if 2 Ts are still alive. Frag hunting during retakes wastes time and gets you killed by crossfires.
knowledge.situation: CT retake, hunting for kills instead of defusing
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: retakes focused on bomb access succeed ~15% more than frag-focused retakes

knowledge.title: Retakes: timing the push — all at once, not staggered
knowledge.description: Retake pushes must be simultaneous. If 3 CTs push at 3 different times, the T handles 3 separate 1v1s and wins each. If all 3 push simultaneously, the T faces a 1v3 crossfire. Count down '3, 2, 1, go' before every retake push.
knowledge.situation: CT retake staggered, losing 1v1s
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: synchronized retakes win ~50%; staggered retakes win ~20%

knowledge.title: Aim: warmup routine — 10 minutes is enough
knowledge.description: Before competitive play, do a 10-minute warmup: 3 minutes aim trainer (flicks), 3 minutes spray control (workshop map), 4 minutes deathmatch (real movement + aim). More than 10 minutes causes fatigue that hurts your first match. Less than 5 minutes means cold aim.
knowledge.situation: Losing first match of the session, cold aim
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 pros warm up 15-30 minutes; for non-pros, 10 minutes captures 90% of the benefit

knowledge.title: Aim: sensitivity consistency matters more than value
knowledge.description: There is no 'correct' sensitivity — what matters is consistency. Pick a sensitivity between 400 DPI / 1.5 in-game and 800 DPI / 1.0 in-game, and never change it. Muscle memory takes 2-3 weeks to build and resets on every change.
knowledge.situation: Constantly changing sensitivity, inconsistent aim
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 pros use sensitivities between 1.0-2.0 at 400 DPI; most stay within a 10% range for years

knowledge.title: CT side: play for information before committing to defense
knowledge.description: In the first 20 seconds of a CT round, gather info without fighting. Send utility for early reads (molly a common T path, listen for footsteps). Only commit to a stack or rotation after you have at least one data point. Blind stacking without info loses ~20% of rounds.
knowledge.situation: CT stacking a site with no information
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 CTs gather info before committing on ~85% of rounds

knowledge.title: T side: default setup wins more than rushing
knowledge.description: On full buy rounds, run a default (spread across the map for info) before committing to a site. Defaults win ~8% more rounds than fast hits because they reveal CT positioning. Rush only on force buys or as a specific counter-strat.
knowledge.situation: T-side always rushing, not defaulting
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 T default rounds win ~52%, fast hits win ~44% on full buys

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: punish predictable CT aggression with patience
knowledge.description: If the CT pushes the same spot aggressively every round, set up a crossfire and wait for them to push into it. Patient play against aggressive CTs converts ~70% of first contacts because the CT is moving into your pre-aimed angles.
knowledge.situation: CT pushing out of the site early every round
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: crossfire traps against predictable CT pushes convert ~70% of first contacts

knowledge.title: Anti-strat: shift from A to B after half-time
knowledge.description: If you won the first half hitting one site, the opponent will adjust. Open the second half hitting the opposite site. The adjustment period (2-3 rounds) gives you free rounds before they re-adapt. Never repeat a half's strategy into the next half.
knowledge.situation: Losing rounds after half-time because the opponent adapted
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: teams that change target site after half-time win the first 2 rounds ~60% of the time

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: deagle eco round discipline
knowledge.description: On eco rounds with a Deagle, hold a close angle and go for one headshot. Do not commit to multiple peeks. The Deagle one-taps at any range but punishes missed first shots with wild inaccuracy. One shot, one kill opportunity — then hide if you miss.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger on eco round with Deagle
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: Deagle first-shot headshot rate is ~25% at tier-1; the weapon punishes spray and rewards patience

knowledge.title: Entry fragger: adapt to CT reads
knowledge.description: If the CT is reading your entry angle (holding exactly where you peek), change your approach. Push a different angle, throw utility from a new position, or go to the other site entirely. Repeating the same entry against an adapted CT is -EV after the second time.
knowledge.situation: Entry fragger dying to the same CT hold repeatedly
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 entry fraggers change approach after 2 consecutive failed entries to the same spot

knowledge.title: AWP: switching to rifle on eco defense
knowledge.description: On CT eco rounds where the team cannot afford utility, consider buying a rifle instead of saving for the AWP. A rifle + utility is more versatile on eco defense than an AWP with no utility. The AWP without smokes or flashes is a sitting target.
knowledge.situation: AWP player on a thin CT economy
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: rifle + full utility wins ~15% more eco defense rounds than AWP alone

knowledge.title: AWP: peek timing vs scope timing
knowledge.description: Scope in BEFORE you peek, not during. The scope animation takes 0.3 seconds — peeking while scoping gives the enemy a free window. Pre-scope around the corner, then step out already zoomed. The difference between pre-scope and mid-peek scope is 200ms of vulnerability.
knowledge.situation: AWP player getting killed while scoping in
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: pre-scoped peeks have ~60% kill rate vs ~35% for mid-peek scope-ins

knowledge.title: Support: when to drop weapons for teammates
knowledge.description: Drop a weapon when: (1) a teammate has a pistol and you have $5000+, (2) the team's star player needs a rifle and you can buy again next round, (3) you are the support player and the entry needs the weapon more. Never drop if it leaves you unable to buy next round.
knowledge.situation: Support player deciding when to drop weapons
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: correct weapon drops raise team round win rate by ~8% on force buys

knowledge.title: Support: information grenades over kill grenades
knowledge.description: Throw utility to gather information, not just to deal damage. A molotov in a common hiding spot tells you if someone is there (they run). A flash that draws no gunfire confirms the angle is empty. Information from utility is worth more than 50 HP of random damage.
knowledge.situation: Support player throwing utility randomly
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: information-driven utility usage correlates with ~10% higher team round win rate

knowledge.title: Lurk: know when to give up the lurk
knowledge.description: If the team is losing rounds because the lurk player is not contributing to the execute, stop lurking and play as the 5th entry. Lurking only works when the team can win 4v5 site hits. If the team needs you on site, be there.
knowledge.situation: Lurk player hurting the team by not being on site
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 lurkers abandon the lurk and join the execute on ~20% of rounds based on situation

knowledge.title: Lurk: delay the enemy rotate, even without kills
knowledge.description: The lurk player does not need kills to be effective. Simply being present on the rotate path forces the CT to slow down, check angles, and arrive at the site 3-5 seconds later. That delay is often enough for the team to plant and set up post-plant.
knowledge.situation: Lurk player judging effectiveness by kill count
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: lurk presence delays CT rotates by 3-5 seconds on average even without engagement

knowledge.title: IGL: read the scoreboard for economy predictions
knowledge.description: Check the scoreboard after every round. A player with 0-0-3 on the enemy team has been saving and likely has $8000+. A player with 15-8 has been dying and may be on a thin buy. Use these reads to predict which site the enemy can utility-stack.
knowledge.situation: IGL not checking scoreboard for economy reads
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: IGLs who track individual enemy economy make correct utility predictions ~75% of the time

knowledge.title: IGL: communicate the plan, not just the call
knowledge.description: Do not just say 'hit A.' Say 'hit A, smoke CT, molly stairs, flash entry, plant default, lurk mid.' The full plan ensures everyone knows their role. Vague calls cause individual improvisation, which leads to desync.
knowledge.situation: IGL giving vague calls, team not coordinating
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: detailed IGL calls with specific roles raise execute success by ~12% vs one-word calls

knowledge.title: Clutch: when to save instead of clutching
knowledge.description: In a 1v3+ with no bomb planted and less than 20 seconds, save the weapon. The economic value of saving a rifle ($2700) outweighs the ~5% chance of winning a 1v3. Clutch only when: (1) bomb is planted, (2) you have kit + time, or (3) you have a realistic path to the bomb.
knowledge.situation: Trying to clutch an unwinnable round instead of saving
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: weapon saves on unwinnable clutch situations recover $2000+ in team economy per save

knowledge.title: Clutch: play the bomb, not the enemy
knowledge.description: In any clutch scenario, the bomb is the objective, not the kills. As T: plant and hide. As CT: reach the bomb and defuse. Fights are only worth taking if they are between you and the bomb. Going hunting wastes time and positions you away from the objective.
knowledge.situation: Clutch player chasing kills instead of playing the objective
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: objective-focused clutch players win ~25% more clutches than frag-focused players

knowledge.title: Positioning: rotate with cover, never in the open
knowledge.description: When rotating between sites, always use the route that provides the most cover. Running through the open saves 2 seconds but gets you killed by the lurker ~15% of the time. The safe rotate path is always worth the extra time.
knowledge.situation: Getting killed during rotation through open areas
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: covered rotation paths reduce rotate deaths by ~60% vs open rotations

knowledge.title: Positioning: the first angle you see is the first angle that sees you
knowledge.description: When moving through the map, the first angle you become visible to is also the first angle you must clear. Pre-aim that angle before you expose yourself. Most mid-round deaths come from not clearing the first visible angle on a rotation or push.
knowledge.situation: Getting killed from unchecked angles while rotating or pushing
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: pre-aiming the first visible angle reduces rotation deaths by ~30%

knowledge.title: Economy: kill reward differences matter
knowledge.description: Rifle kills award $300, SMG kills $600, shotgun kills $900, knife kills $1500. On anti-eco, consider using an SMG for the double kill reward. 5 SMG kills on anti-eco = $3000 extra team economy, equivalent to a free rifle for a teammate next round.
knowledge.situation: Not considering kill rewards in weapon choice
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams use SMGs on anti-eco ~30% of the time for the $600 kill reward

knowledge.title: Economy: second-round buy after winning pistol
knowledge.description: After winning pistol, buy SMG + armor (not a rifle). The SMG $600 kill reward maximizes economic damage to the enemy. The enemy is on eco — they have pistols. An SMG kills pistol players just as fast as a rifle does, but earns twice the money.
knowledge.situation: Buying rifle on round 2 after winning pistol
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: tier-1 teams buy SMGs in 90%+ of second rounds after winning pistol

knowledge.title: Utility: smoke extinguish — molotov through smoke
knowledge.description: In CS2, throwing a smoke on a molotov extinguishes the fire. If the enemy mollies your position, throw a smoke to put it out. This costs $300 to counter a $400 molotov — economically favorable and lets you hold your position.
knowledge.situation: Getting mollied out of position with no counter
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: smoke extinguishing molotovs is a standard CS2 mechanic used on ~15% of defensive mollies

knowledge.title: Utility: bounce flash for pop flash effect
knowledge.description: Throw the flash so it bounces off a wall or floor and pops the instant it becomes visible to the enemy. A pop flash gives the enemy zero reaction time to turn away. Practice wall-bounce flashes for every major choke point — the bounce angle determines the pop timing.
knowledge.situation: Throwing flashes that enemies can easily avoid
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: pop flashes (< 0.3s visible) fully blind enemies; high-arc flashes (> 1s visible) blind <40%

knowledge.title: Aim: first bullet accuracy — stand still
knowledge.description: In CS2, the first bullet is perfectly accurate only when you are completely still. Moving, even at walk speed, adds spread. Counter-strafe to a complete stop before shooting. The difference between still and walking first-bullet accuracy is ~5x spread at 20 meters.
knowledge.situation: Missing first bullets while moving
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: standing still first-bullet accuracy is ~95% at 20m; walking is ~65%

knowledge.title: Aim: burst fire at medium range
knowledge.description: At 15-30 meters, burst fire (2-4 bullets) is more effective than spray or tap. The first 2-4 bullets of the AK/M4 follow a predictable vertical pattern — pull down slightly and reset. Full spray at medium range wastes ammo and gives away your position.
knowledge.situation: Missing at medium range, full spraying
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: 2-4 bullet bursts have ~70% accuracy at 20m vs ~35% for full spray

knowledge.title: Positioning: play one angle at a time
knowledge.description: Never expose yourself to two angles simultaneously. Clear one angle, then reposition to clear the next. If you can be shot from two directions at once, you lose no matter how good your aim is. Isolating angles is the core of smart positioning.
knowledge.situation: Dying to crossfires, exposed to multiple angles
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: players who isolate angles win ~65% of duels; players exposed to 2+ angles win ~30%

knowledge.title: Positioning: verticality — use elevation when available
knowledge.description: Higher positions win duels because the enemy must look up (unnatural crosshair placement). On any map where you can get elevation (boxes, ledges, scaffolding), use it. The first 1-2 uses of a new elevation spot are essentially free kills.
knowledge.situation: Always playing ground-level angles
knowledge.pro_example: Pro meta: elevation positions win first contact ~62% vs ~48% for ground-level on equal-skill duels
